title: Matrix Inversion
summary: How to find the inverse of 2×2 and n×n matrices, and apply it to solve linear systems.
content_html:
What is the Inverse?

The inverse of square matrix A, denoted A⁻¹, satisfies: A·A⁻¹ = A⁻¹·A = I

An inverse exists if and only if det(A) ≠ 0 (A is non-singular).

Inverse of a 2×2 Matrix

For A = [[a,b],[c,d]] with det(A) = ad - bc ≠ 0:

A⁻¹ = (1/det(A)) × [ d  -b]
                    [-c   a]

Example: A = [[3,2],[1,4]], det = 12−2 = 10

A⁻¹ = (1/10) × [4  -2] = [0.4  -0.2]
               [-1   3]   [-0.1  0.3]

Inverse of n×n Matrix — Gauss-Jordan Method

Form the augmented matrix [A|I] and apply row operations until the left side becomes I. The right side will be A⁻¹.

[A | I] → row operations → [I | A⁻¹]

Example: Find inverse of A = [[1,2],[3,4]]

[1  2 | 1  0]   R2-3R1→  [1  2 | 1  0]
[3  4 | 0  1]            [0 -2 |-3  1]

(-1/2)R2→  [1  2 | 1    0  ]
           [0  1 | 3/2 -1/2]

R1-2R2→  [1  0 | -2   1  ]
         [0  1 |  3/2 -1/2]

A⁻¹ = [-2    1  ]
      [ 3/2 -1/2]

Solving Ax = b using the Inverse

If A⁻¹ exists, then the unique solution is: x = A⁻¹b

Properties of Inverse

  • (A⁻¹)⁻¹ = A
  • (AB)⁻¹ = B⁻¹A⁻¹ (reversed order)
  • (Aᵀ)⁻¹ = (A⁻¹)ᵀ
  • (kA)⁻¹ = (1/k)A⁻¹

exercises.answer: Row-reducing [A|I] until left side becomes I
